data.title: TSLW: Big Payouts While Holding NAV
data.description:
The Roundhill TSLA WeeklyPay ETF (TSLW), according to the company's description, is built to provide investors with the potential to generate income and growth.

Roundhill's goal is to pay out distributions on a weekly basis of "1.2 times the total return of Tesla common shares." Other than a distribution of $0.1693 paid out on 1-13-2026, the distribution has been above $0.20 per share, with the majority of them being over $0.31 per share.

For an ETF using leverage as TSLW does, this is an impressive showing on the income side of the equation. Even so, the distributions can significantly vary from week-to-week. When taken on average together, the monthly totals reflect a successful execution of its strategy.

Its share price has been volatile over the last year, ranging from a 52-week low of $25.45 to a 52-week high or $51.90.

In this article we'll look at the strengths and weaknesses of TSLW and what its future looks like for investors.

The underlying

A single-stock ETF like TSLW will perform as well as the underlying, which in this case is Tesla (TSLA). How one views Tesla will determine the expected performance of TSLW going forward.

There's not much to say about Tesla that isn't already known, but the key thing to consider as it relates to TSLW is the overall areas it competes in. For example, AI, energy generation, energy storage, EVs, leasing, and robotics.

With a PE (FWD) of 275, it's obvious that the valuation of the company is based upon future growth expectations, which have been driving up the share price of the company since April 2025, when on April 7, 2025 it hit its 52-week low of $214.25, and has more than doubled to $448.00 per share as I write.

What's interesting about the performance of TSLW is it hasn't participated in the upside movement of TSLA's share price on a consistent basis but has rather moved in a wide range during that time; not holding the top, it has jumped to.

For that reason, even though Roundhill describes the ETF as a growth and income vehicle, I see it primarily as an income-generating holding. Now it wouldn't surprise me if TSLW were to gradually climb in the future along with the upward price growth trajectory of TSLA, but it would probably be at a more modest pace, sustainably lagging behind the underlying.

Also, with the 1.2x leverage, it would seem if it was working well, it would provide a higher floor and ceiling during the time TSLA has been soaring in value.

A look at the chart

While TSLW hasn't been able to consistently and sustainably capture the upside of TSLA's growth over the last year, it has been doing very well on the income side.

For those not familiar with charts associated with high-yielding ETFs like TSLW, the one below is actually a very good one, considering the major purpose of the holding.

The performance reflected in the chart confirms that even with the 1.2x leverage, it hasn't resulted in the overall share price dropping below its floor. And as mentioned earlier, the same goes on the ceiling, where it hasn't been able to break through there as well.

My point is, for an ETF generating the type of income TSLW is, to trade sideways with some volatility, while maintaining its price floor, is a solid performance.

In my research, if an ETF like TSLW is able to trade sideways or slightly above NAV, it's doing the job it was designed to do. The chart below is what most successful high-yield ETFs should look like when they're healthy.

There are some that vastly outperform on share price and income generated, such as the YieldMax Semiconductor Portfolio Option Income ETF (CHPY), which I wrote about recently, but ETFs like that are anomalies, and not the general norm.

What the chart shows is TSLW is more than able to hold NAV while it produces significant income for the investor.

TSLW utilizes swaps with weekly exposure, which is different than its competitors that incorporate daily swaps as part of their income and growth strategy.

Risks

Since the inception of the ETF, we have some understanding as to how the fund performs under optimal conditions, considering the 1.2x leverage it utilizes.

What has yet to be seen is how TSLW will perform when TSLA corrects. We know it is very volatile, jumping in ranges of approximately $8.00 per share to $20.00 per share, and falling back again. Based upon that historical record, it would suggest that when TSLA eventually corrects for a sustainable period of time, it would likely result in the ETF participating in that decline, where the drop in share price could be deeper than when it falls in the TSLA growth period.

But if TSLA continues to grow over the next year or so in share price, the inevitable correction could come from a higher price point, which could mean TSLW may outperform what it has done over the last year, based upon a higher floor.

Where the challenge would probably come would be in regard to its distribution yield, which would drop in conjunction with a price correction of TSLA.

But again, it depends upon how high TSLA rises before that happens, and what the new floor will be with TSLW's share price.

My thought is that TSLW would correct and then trade sideways as it has been during the recent growth trajectory of TSLA. Once TSLA recovers, we'll probably see a similar trading pattern for TSLW as we have seen in the past.

The caveat there is if or when TSLA's promising products start to increase exponentially in demand, which could result in some significant upside for TSLW's growth and income.

Conclusion

I like how TSLW has been performing over the last year or so, especially in relation to its distribution yield. I expect the yield to continue to be volatile, and for the ETF to continue trading sideways to a lot of volatility.

As noted earlier, the chart of TSLW is a good one for what it is designed to do, and those unfamiliar with high-yield ETFs like TSLW should know that this is how charts for these types of securities will look, with the occasional exception to the rule.

If an investor believes in the future of TSLA, then TSLW should be an ETF to take a close look at. I think the growth of TSLA will be extraordinary over the long haul, and those riding that with an income-generating ETF like TSLW, should do very well.

The key is to first, understand what the ETF is and does in order to not be chased out of it because of the volatility, which will continue to be part of the experience of holding it. Those that panic sell can suffer unrealized losses because of not embracing the fact that this is the price that comes with receiving much higher yields than most of the market offers.

data.title: VBK: Cheap Small-Cap Growth Exposure, But Risks Are High
data.description:
The Vanguard Small-Cap Growth Index Fund ETF Shares (VBK), launched on 01/26/2004 and managed by The Vanguard Group, Inc., provides exposure to stocks within the small-cap segment of the equity market with growth characteristics. It manages ~$39 billion and charges a 0.07% expense ratio.

This fund is efficient as far as its approach is concerned because the methodology is fundamentally-driven and it has the lowest expense ratio among peers, making it an attractive vehicle for long-term exposure. However, the current risks do not warrant exposure to the market; it gives you access to.

Methodology

VBK tracks the CRSP US Small Cap Growth Index, which uses the analysts' estimates of long-term and short-term EPS growth rates, the investment/assets ratio, ROA, plus historical 3-year growth in earnings and sales. The index is market-cap-weighted, and the reconstitution/rebalancing happens every quarter.

This is an interesting set of metrics because I usually encounter more simplistic approaches to defining growth, such as using value metrics like earnings yield and then selecting those stocks with the lowest values. Instead of assuming that the most overvalued stocks in a market segment would be the fastest-growing, CRSP does the actual work of finding those stocks that have growth traits.

Another thing to note is that it's more fundamentally-driven. We have investment into the business and profitability taken into account on top of expected/historical growth, factors that are very important for growth potential. Momentum as a factor is missing, and yet it is common in growth funds. The index doesn't conflate growth potential and historical price movement into its growth definition.

Portfolio

As is usual with small-cap portfolios, the idiosyncratic risks are negligible here since the top 10 holdings account for just under 10% of the portfolio, and the top holding has only a weight of 1.44%. Though the top holdings are not significant contributors, for what it's worth, the makeup here indicates exposure to long-duration growth assets; for example, biotech/medtech through Insmed Inc. (INSM) and Natera (NTRA), as well as tech through Ciena (CIEN), Lumentum (LITE), Coherent (COHR), Credo (CRDO), and Pure Storage (PSTG).

What's more useful for assessing the sensitivity to macroeconomic factors is the sector distribution. And it confirms a bias towards long duration as Tech stocks account for nearly one third of the fund; not very different from the current bias of the broad market. With Industrials right behind at ~20% and Consumer Cyclical having an ~11% weight, the cyclical element is strong, but there is also a significant contribution by healthcare stocks, which are appreciated in a highly uncertain environment such as this.

Alternatives

If you're looking for the cheapest way to get exposure to small-cap growth stocks, VBK has the lowest expense ratio. The Vanguard S&P Small-Cap 600 Growth Index Fund ETF Shares (VIOG) is close at 0.10% per annum, but incorporates momentum as a factor into determining growth. If someone, for whatever reason, prefers the more classic ways to approach growth investing, the iShares Russell 2000 Growth ETF (IWO) is another prominent choice, but it is way more expensive, charging 0.24%. Speaking of expensive, I should also mention the existence of the Invesco S&P SmallCap 600 Pure Growth ETF (RZG), which incorporates the same factors as VIOG (sales growth, earnings change to price, momentum), but is more concentrated and weights based on growth score. It has an expense ratio of 0.35%.

Performance

Over the long run, VBK has performed very similarly to its peers and actually outperformed the more expensive RZG to a meaningful degree.

Another thing that stands out is that the still cheap VIOG has been outperforming the rest for the last 5 years, by partially realizing a less severe drawdown back in 2022.

In fact, VIOG's worst year was represented by a meaningfully lower loss in the last 10 years.

Now, the dilemma is whether a bias towards small-cap growth is warranted given the past underperformance relative to the broader market as represented by the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY).

Risks

The most likely scenario I have outlined in past articles is growth deceleration coupled with continued Fed Funds rate cuts as inflation has moderated, but recession risk is now higher as the labor market has softened materially in 2025. This is positive for small-cap growth relative to the broader market because growth stocks are usually long-duration assets that meaningfully benefit from lower rates in the short run, and small-cap stocks are more dependent on borrowing costs than large-cap ones.

However, if earnings breadth remains narrow, even in such a regime, the broad market can keep outperforming small-cap growth because the current market structure is biased towards long-duration assets too, with the advantage of mega-cap inclusion. That represents a potential opportunity cost worth considering.

Of course, I need to also mention that the inflation trajectory has been anything but promising in 2025. It recently declined, but only after climbing above 3% in 2025. Tariffs are still expected to keep it sticky in 2026. If real yield expectations are adjusted because of this risk materializing, small-cap growth should be hit harder than the broad market.

Another thing to note is that small-cap growth is relatively expensive, and VBK is no exception. As of year-end 2025, the earnings multiple is 31x, and it has a price-to-book ratio of 4.6x. These are close to the numbers large-cap ETFs have these days. This is a longer-term risk because if economic growth remains low, the current valuation and earnings potential imply a cap on total returns for investors. This is a big opportunity risk for U.S. equities, and it's an even bigger risk for small-cap growth stocks.

Conclusion

So, in conclusion, VBK is an inexpensive and efficient way to get exposure to the small-cap growth equity market, but the risks don't warrant a bias towards them for now. I am rating VBK a hold.

Now, I am always open to challenging my views because being an ideologue in investing and trading can prove expensive. So if you have a different opinion, this platform is the right place to discuss both strategy and the various options with which we can position our portfolios. Go ahead and leave a comment below to let me know what you think.

Otherwise, please let me know if you found this article useful. It means a lot!

data.title: RingCentral: Willing To Bite At Low Prices As New Products Show Promise
data.description:
In today's market, almost every software company has rebranded itself as an AI company. RingCentral (RNG), a company known for contact center solutions and employee/customer experience tools, is no different. Over the past year, the company has taken great pains to rebrand itself as the AI Voice company, selling AI communications-in-a-box functionalities primarily for smaller businesses.

Wall Street, needless to say, has been more skeptical on this conversion. The stock is down more than 20% over the past year as RingCentral has struggled with overall growth. While new products are showcasing promise, most of this business is being converted over from old products, and top-line growth is still sitting in the mid single digits. But at the same time, we do have to recognize the fact that RingCentral may have dropped to values that are too low to ignore.

I'm initiating RingCentral at a buy rating. While I don't think this is anywhere close to the highest-quality software or AI company on the market, I'm increasingly more wary of expensive valuation multiples in the market. I think RingCentral's combination of steady growth, rising operating margins, and healthy cash flow will be great assets from an investment perspective as the market potentially rotates away from expensive large-cap names and into small-cap value.

The AI Voice Platform

First, let's get a solid understanding of what RingCentral does. The company was founded in 1999 as a business contact center solution. Its core, original use case was to migrate businesses off traditional phone lines and into modernized contact centers that ran through the cloud. On top of this, the company also offered products for internal communications, via both messaging and video (similar to Slack or Microsoft Teams).

Of course, in today's IT landscape, contact centers themselves are getting outdated. This is why RingCentral made the pivot to advertise its agentic AI solutions. Now, the company is touting the ability to give its customers plug-and-play AI agents for each touchpoint with customers and employees.

The snapshot below showcases the breadth of RingCentral's new product portfolio. Its flagship new product is AI Receptionist, which the company launched just recently in February 2025. The receptionist handles communications with customers, captures leads and books appointments, and handles all queries in multiple languages.

The AI virtual assistant product, meanwhile, is able to automate many repetitive workflows (such as onboarding new customers or employees), while also being able to summarize notes and action items from meetings.

The company also has a tool called "Conversation Expert," which captures audio data from recorded calls to run analytics and spot insights. It purports to be able to spot churn risks from customer feedback, while coaching human sales agents in order to improve their close performance.

RingCentral believes that its new AI tools have vastly expanded its market opportunity. Now, adding on AI CX and conversational intelligence tools has expanded its CAGR by an additional ~$65 billion, adding on top of its existing UCaaS (unified communications as a service) product capabilities.

Also worth noting is the fact that RingCentral has a major sales partnership with AT&T (T). AT&T Business Customers are able to access RingCentral's contact center and conversational intelligence products from within their AT&T subscriptions, widening RingCentral's sales net to a broader pool of SMBs.

AI products are showcasing expanded ARR, but at the expense of legacy tools

Let's now dig into more of the recent trends that RingCentral is exhibiting. The chart below showcases key metrics for the company's three major AI products: AI receptionist, RingCX, and AI Conversation Expert. All of these recently-launched products in 2025 are showcasing rapid growth. AI receptionist, in particular (which launched in February), nearly doubled its active customers in one quarter, going from Q2 to Q3.

By the end of 2025, RingCentral aims to achieve over $100 million in ARR (annualized recurring revenue) across all of its new AI products, roughly doubling over $50 million in FY24.

Now, this is where we also need to be realistic. While the AI portion of the business is what RingCentral highlights, we note that overall top-line growth isn't terribly impressive. The company's guidance for FY25 only calls for 4.5-5.0% y/y growth; revenue in Q3 itself only grew 5% y/y, with slightly stronger 6% y/y growth in subscription revenue. This suggests that RingCentral is doing a good job at converting its existing customers from legacy products onto new ones, potentially increasing average spend, but all in all, AI isn't a truly meaningful, earth-shattering growth accelerator for the company. Rather, AI is just a means for RingCentral to stay in the game.

That said, we do like that RingCentral isn't merely fading away, but is also expanding its margin profile as it works to do a 180-pivot on its products. Pro forma operating margins this year are expected to rise 150bps y/y to 22.5%, while free cash flow margins are also expected to hit 21.0%, with the implied ~$528 million in midpoint FCF this year growing >30% y/y. So despite potentially meager overall top-line growth, a >30% growth clip in FCF isn't something that we can ignore in this market, especially when the stock trades at such cheap multiples.

We like the fact as well that RingCentral has deployed its rising FCF to deleverage itself consistently over time. As of the most recent quarter, RingCentral's net debt fell to $1.11 billion, representing a relatively modest 1.7x leverage ratio against trailing adjusted EBITDA, down 0.8x versus a 2.5x leverage ratio at the same point last year.

Cheap valuation may set up RingCentral for potential acquisition

Now, we can arrive at the meat of why buying RingCentral makes sense, even if it's not the most exciting AI stock on the block. At current share prices near $27, RingCentral trades at a $2.30 billion market cap. Tacking on the $1.11 billion of net debt on its latest balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $3.41 billion.

For the remainder of FY25, RingCentral has guided to ~5% y/y revenue growth, yielding $4.29-$4.33 in pro forma EPS and $525-$530 million in FCF at a ~21% FCF margin.

For FY26, Wall Street is expecting RingCentral to generate $2.62 billion in revenue (+4% y/y) and hit $4.76 in pro forma EPS (+11% y/y). If we conservatively assume this year's 21% FCF margins hold, FCF in FY26 would be $550 million. This positions RingCentral's valuation multiples at:

  • 6.5x EV/FY25 FCF and 6.2x EV/FY26 FCF
  • 6.3x FY25 P/E and 5.7x FY26 P/E

These are difficult valuation multiples to ignore. With RingCentral boasting a recurring-revenue base and rapidly rising ARR in AI products, I see the company as a potential acquisition target, especially by a larger legacy company (such as existing partner AT&T) that is looking to strengthen its enterprise solutions with out-of-the-box software capabilities. But even on a standalone basis, a single-digit FCF multiple won't last long in the market, especially if growth remains on a consistent track.

Q4 look-ahead, risks, and key takeaways

The next major catalyst for RingCentral is its Q4 earnings release, expected in early/mid-February. To me, the key catalysts that will move the stock are:

  • FY26 outlook. RingCentral is expected to unveil its first pass at its 2026 outlook. Wall Street's expectations, as previously mentioned, aren't too aggressive, calling for just 4% y/y growth. But we're looking out in particular for the company's comments around how much it expects new AI product ARR growth to be in FY25, and how that will interplay against declines in legacy products.
  • Margin expansion amid AI usage. RingCentral has been adamant about leveraging its own AI products to slice headcount out of its operating structure, which is helping to drive the consistent operating margin expansion over the past few years (many larger and more prominent software companies like ServiceNow (NOW), in my view, fail at proving that it's actually effective at eliminating its corporate bureaucracy using its own AI solutions, as their headcount continues to balloon). We're hoping for at least ~200bps of further operating margin expansion in FY26 to help build support for the narrative that RingCentral is committed to growing its cash flow.

Of course, there are many risks for us to keep in mind, which is why RingCentral trades at cheap multiples. To me, competition is the biggest red flag. Let's face it: none of RingCentral's products are unique. Many companies have released AI voice assistants and "copilot"-type AI products that make intelligent recommendations and run analytics on internal data. We think Twilio (TWLO) is a major competitor in the communications-as-a-service software space, while SoundHound (SOUN) also specializes in voice AI agents and has a lock on certain industries, especially foodservice and hospitality. The company's VoIP (voice over internet protocol) legacy business and internal communications tools are also overshadowed by the likes of Microsoft Teams and Slack. We note as well that RingCentral has a large exposure to SMB and mid-market customers. Of its total $2.6 billion in ARR, only $1 billion are true enterprise customers (which the company loosely defines as companies that have more than 100 employees, which I think is quite a generous definition of an enterprise).

That said, we've already acknowledged that RingCentral isn't the most exciting or highest-quality AI/software stock in the market, and everything must be taken in the context of its mid-single-digit FCF multiples. In today's market, it's rare to find such a low valuation for a company that is still growing and has meaningful catalysts to continue driving growth: as such, I believe risk to be priced in.

While I wouldn't bet the proverbial farm here, RingCentral is worth a small buy.

data.title: Companhia Siderúrgica Nacional: Too Much Debt For Too Little Margin Of Error
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Companhia Siderúrgica Nacional (SID), or simply CSN, does not look like a stock I want to be long right now. Not because the company is bad or lacks relevant assets, but because the level of risk is simply too high for this stage of the cycle.

The company carries a very heavy debt load, roughly R$ 37 billion, with leverage close to 4x EBITDA. For a cyclical business, that alone is already a major issue. It becomes even more problematic when operations are not helping, margins are under pressure, and results depend heavily on the commodity cycle.

At this point, the entire bullish case for CSN is based on events that have not yet happened. Asset sales, meaningful deleveraging, a sharp reduction in net debt, and a repricing of the stock. If all of that materializes, the story changes. But right now, this is still a plan, not reality.

Until these asset sales actually take place, investors remain exposed to a highly leveraged company with very little margin for error. If iron ore prices fall, results suffer. If steel margins remain pressured, EBITDA does not grow. And with high leverage, any disappointment flows directly into the stock price.

On top of that, this is not a stock for income-oriented investors. The company itself has made it clear that the priority is deleveraging. In past cycles, CSN went years without paying dividends. Anyone buying the stock expecting recurring income is likely to be disappointed.

Lastly, history shows that cyclical stocks like CSN can deliver very strong returns when the cycle turns. Between 2016 and the 2021 peak, for example, the stock delivered roughly 1,500% in total returns. At the same time, it can destroy value when investors enter at the wrong moment. In 2021, many investors bought near the top, with the stock close to R$ 50, only to see it fall by more than 80% afterward.

Given the current setup, I prefer to stay on the sidelines. The risk-reward does not look attractive at this point. Until deleveraging becomes a fact rather than a promise, the asymmetry remains unfavorable.

In my view, the appropriate positioning today is SELL.

A Bit About the Company

Companhia Siderúrgica Nacional is a Brazilian-based materials company, with operations primarily focused on steelmaking and mining, but also with relevant businesses in logistics, energy, and cement.

In practical terms, CSN is a highly integrated group. It produces both flat and long steel products, extracts iron ore, and owns its own logistics assets—such as railroads and ports—which help both in shipping production and reducing operating costs. Over the years, the company has also expanded into areas like cement and power generation, building a diversified portfolio within the industrial sector.

In the United States, the company trades through ADRs under the ticker SID, making it one of the main vehicles for foreign investors seeking exposure to the Brazilian steel sector. Currently, its market capitalization stands at roughly US$ 2.2 billion.

CSN is highly sensitive to economic cycles and commodity prices, especially steel and iron ore. When the cycle is favorable, results tend to improve sharply. When conditions turn, the impact on the balance sheet is usually fast and intense—something that helps explain why the stock goes through long periods of high volatility.

Financials That Pose Serious Risks

Results Still Highly Dependent on the Cycle

When I look at CSN’s results, it becomes clear that they remain heavily dependent on the commodity cycle.

The company itself works with aggressive growth projections, even talking about doubling results over the next eight years. But all of that depends on several assumptions. If iron ore prices rise or fall sharply, future results can end up much weaker or much stronger than what is currently projected. In other words, the company’s financial performance remains fully exposed to the cycle.

In steelmaking, the picture is also not favorable. Margins are under pressure, mainly due to increased competition, the inflow of lower-priced Chinese products, and weaker steel prices. This segment represents roughly half of the company’s revenue but contributes only about 18% of EBITDA, which clearly shows how pressured profitability has become.

CSN Mineração Supports a Large Share of EBITDA

One of CSN’s key operations is CSN Mineração, which trades on the Brazilian exchange under the ticker CMIN3. For context, CSN Mineração is the seventh-largest iron ore exporter in the world, while Vale (VALE) ranks among the largest, with roughly 20% global market share.

Today, CSN Mineração is the main profit engine of the group. It represents about 33% of total revenue but accounts for roughly 57% of consolidated EBITDA. This highlights how dependent the group has become on iron ore to sustain overall results.

Another important point is the company’s track record of operational records, driven by strong cash generation and high returns. CSN Mineração also benefits from a long mine life, with approximately 2.5 billion tons of reserves. If production were to stabilize at around 60 million tons per year, those reserves would last close to 40 years.

Even if production increases by roughly 40%, the reserve life would still exceed 40 years. From a very long-term perspective, this suggests the business will remain relevant for decades.

There is also the P15 expansion project, which could increase production by about 40% by 2030. According to the company, this expansion could lift mining EBITDA from roughly R$ 6.7 billion to R$ 9.8 billion, representing growth of around 46%. Once again, however, all of this depends directly on iron ore prices.

High Debt Continues to Pressure Financial Results

The main issue is that even when results improve, they still need to support a highly leveraged capital structure. Today, CSN’s net debt stands at around R$ 37.5 billion, with leverage close to 3.1x EBITDA.

At this level of indebtedness, financial expenses weigh heavily on results. The company itself makes it clear that, without a meaningful reduction in debt, it is difficult to unlock value in a consistent way.

Cash generation alone is not enough to solve this problem in the short term. That is why the strategy necessarily involves asset sales. Until that happens, financial performance remains under pressure.

Infrastructure, Cement, and Energy

CSN Infraestrutura has been delivering steadily growing results over time. The company believes that EBITDA from this segment could grow from roughly R$ 1.7 billion to more than R$ 10 billion in the future. This is a relevant asset that could generate significant cash if partially sold.

In CSN Cimentos, the operation represents around 10% to 11% of group revenue. The plan is to sell control of this business starting in 2026, specifically to help with deleveraging.

CSN also has an energy division, with 27 hydro assets, three cogeneration thermal plants, three wind projects, and one solar project. Installed capacity is approximately 2 GW. For each 1 MW of capacity, the company would need to invest around R$ 5 million.

In other words, replicating the current capacity would require roughly R$ 10 billion in investments. This highlights the relevance of these assets, particularly in ensuring energy self-sufficiency, which CSN has achieved since 2023.

Addressing Valuation, Deleveraging, and Dividends

The path to deleveraging runs through the sale of part of CSN Infraestrutura and the sale of control of CSN Cimentos. Financial institutions are already engaged in these transactions. The expectation is that the sale of CSN Infraestrutura could take place by the end of this year, while the sale of CSN Cimentos is expected in the second half of 2026.

As part of its closing remarks, the company emphasized its focus on business profitability, maximizing cash flow generation, a sharp reduction in leverage and gross debt, as well as disciplined and efficient capital allocation, with a strict leverage target.

If these asset sales materialize, net debt could fall from R$ 37.5 billion to R$ 19.5 billion, while EBITDA could decline from R$ 12 billion to R$ 10.7 billion. Leverage would drop from 3.1x to 1.8x EBITDA. In addition, interest expenses would be reduced by roughly R$ 1.5 billion to R$ 1.8 billion per year, solely due to the lower debt level.

Running a simple calculation, with net debt of R$ 19.5 billion and EBITDA of R$ 10.7 billion, enterprise value would be around R$ 33 billion. Today, CSN’s market capitalization is roughly R$ 13.6 billion. That would imply the company trading at close to 3x EV/EBITDA, a level that is historically low outside of the 2021 cycle peak.

That is why many investors look at the stock today and see value. The interpretation is that, if deleveraging happens, the market could reprice the company, since the balance sheet would become lighter and financial costs would fall materially.

The problem is that this “cheap” valuation depends on one key condition: asset sales must actually happen. Without them, the company continues to carry high debt and elevated leverage, and, in my view, the low multiple reflects risk, not opportunity.

In other words, the stock does not look cheap because the environment is favorable. It looks cheap because the market is discounting a high level of uncertainty. Until deleveraging becomes a fact, valuation alone does not support the thesis.

Finally, it is important to highlight that CSN is not a dividend stock in the short term. The company’s stated priority is deleveraging. Historically, when dividends are paid, they tend to be semiannual, usually in April and November. However, in several periods—such as between 2016 and 2018, during the pandemic, and in 2025—the company paid no dividends at all.

In My View, Not Worth It

What CSN shows today is a company with relevant assets, large scale, and potential for improvement, but one that carries a level of risk that is simply too high for the current environment.

Results remain highly dependent on the cycle, steel margins are under pressure, and debt is still elevated. Any more positive interpretation relies on events that have not yet happened, especially asset sales and an effective reduction in leverage.

The valuation may look cheap, but this discount reflects uncertainty. Until deleveraging becomes a fact, a low multiple is not a guarantee of opportunity. It is simply the market demanding a higher risk premium.

There are clear catalysts that could change this story, such as the sale of CSN Infrastructure, the sale of CSN Cimentos, and a more favorable iron ore cycle. But until those materialize, investors remain exposed to a highly leveraged company with very little margin for error.

That is why my view remains unchanged. Under the current conditions, CSN is not a stock worth holding in a portfolio. The risk does not justify the exposure, and the thesis remains SELL.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Duolingo Speaks The Language Of Long-Term Compounding
data.description:
Introduction

Duolingo (DUOL) is the leader in language teaching and a household name that needs no introduction. As a highly profitable and rapidly growing consumer tech app, Duolingo has long enjoyed a large premium. That has finally changed, and the market is now allowing Duolingo to be purchased at a reasonably cheap price for arguably the first time since its IPO. While other household consumer tech subscription giants are facing tough competition, Duolingo operates as a relatively unique business with surprisingly little direct competition at scale. Thus, I am bullish on DUOL.

AI is an Accelerator

Duolingo is one of many stocks selling off due to AI advancement fears. These fears are reasonable, so I'll break them down first. Companies like Chegg (CHGG) are getting cannibalized by ChatGPT, and people are using Claude to make SaaS apps in mere hours. This leaves obvious concerns:

"Duolingo can be easily copied." The first layer to this is translation. Anyone who uses Instagram's translation feature or even Google Translate for languages like Korean understands that right now even the biggest companies with the most data in the world struggle to provide a high-quality translation. From my personal experiences, I would say about 10% of the time Instagram's translation feature is completely inaccurate. This is just mere translation, not even learning. I do believe that AI will get to the point of near-perfect translation, but even when that happens, there is the next step of learning.

Learning has all the pitfalls of human emotion, so Duolingo has quite a large upper hand on AI advancement in this regard. However, it is not impossible that this stage is also reached, but it still doesn't matter. Even if a competitor were to replicate Duolingo near perfectly, distribution would be a massive challenge. Duolingo benefits from being the pioneer, the network effect, and user habits already in place. An example of the distribution challenge would be Meta (META) trying to acquire Manus. Meta could easily build Manus as it is simply a wrapper; however, they still tried to acquire it just because of distribution. Just like high-end watches or luxury purses, near-perfect replicas do exist, but they don't have many users since users always prefer the original brand. Ultimately, not many people want to invest time and energy in a knockoff.

"People will use ChatGPT or another generative AI to learn another language instead of Duolingo." This concern is pretty easy to refute. While generative AI may be good for the occasional question, Duolingo provides a structured learning system and even has tests accepted by universities. Also, most people familiar with Duolingo know it is very gamified and addicting. Duolingo makes learning fun, and it would be difficult for LLMs to achieve that. Just like how people will get a personal trainer when basically everything is available online to learn how to exercise, motivation and professionalism will always be valuable.

Getting past these common concerns, I would like to say that AI is an accelerator for Duolingo. AI simply allows people to do more with less, and Duolingo can pair its massive amounts of user data with AI to rapidly improve and bring on new languages. It can also expand into other games and has even made a move by adding chess lessons last year! In CEO Luis von Ahn's own words:

I see it [AI] as a tool to accelerate what we do, at the same or better level of quality... My goal is for Duos to feel empowered and prepared to use this technology.

Financials & Balance Sheet

Looking at Duolingo's income and cash flow statements, two main things stand out: interest income and stock-based compensation expense. As of last quarter, interest income was $11.8MM and operating income was $35.2MM, so interest income was equivalent to 33.5% of operating income. While some may consider this valid income, I think it is important to only consider Duolingo's core business since this is not scalable. During the same timeframe, stock-based compensation was $35.6MM compared to free cash flow of $77.4MM or 46% of free cash flow. I've always felt stock-based compensation is a real and nasty expense, so I also considered that in my valuation section later in the article.

Moving forward to Duolingo's balance sheet, there is nothing very interesting, but it is obvious it's strong with over a billion dollars in cash and no debt. Their high-interest income aligns with this, and it makes sense.

Valuation & Price Target

Duolingo's valuation story is surprisingly complicated. On one hand, a bull can point to the GAAP P/E or EV/FCF and say that Duolingo is dirt cheap since both are less than 20. However, a bear can make the argument that the GAAP P/E is artificially low due to a one-time tax benefit and EV/FCF is not an applicable method due to the bloated SBC. One can also look at the EBITDA and say it's sky-high. No one is necessarily wrong, but I think the valuation adjustments I put together best represent Duolingo's current situation.

My preferred valuation for Duolingo is an adjusted EV/FCF that only considers Duolingo's core business. To do this, I used Duolingo's fully diluted market capitalization of $7.3B and removed the $1B of cash and cash equivalents, $109.9MM of short-term investments, and added $93.3MM of long-term obligation under operating leases. This gave me an EV of $6.3B. Starting with the $347.6MM of TTM free cash flow Duolingo reported, the first thing I did was remove stock-based compensation of $131MM since I consider it to be a real expense. Since I removed the cash to get EV earlier, and also want to consider only Duolingo's core business, I removed interest income of $44.3MM next. Those adjustments gave me a core operations FCF of $172.3MM, on an EV of $6.3B. This allowed me to reach an adjusted EV/FCF of 36.5 based on the January 21, 2026, closing price. While this does not seem cheap at first glance, the 38% expected topline growth and the 86% adjusted free cash flow growth for 2025 make up for this.

To look at peer valuation, I chose Spotify (SPOT) rather than a language learning company. I think this is a more valuable comparison since Spotify has a freemium business model like Duolingo and also is a consumer tech household name like Duolingo. Doing the same free cash flow adjustments on Spotify that I did to Duolingo, you get an adjusted EV/FCF of 40.9 with less than a third of the topline growth and a little bit over half of the adjusted FCF growth. Spotify has direct competitors like Apple Music and YouTube Premium, while Duolingo does not. There are many apps to learn languages, but only Duolingo makes it that gamified. Those selling points make Duolingo look very attractive. Duolingo is cheaper while growing much faster, with minimal direct competition.

Since Duolingo is a high-quality and durable business, I believe a TTM adjusted EV/FCF multiple equivalent to the 86% adjusted free cash flow growth is reasonable. This is essentially a PEG ratio of 1, but with EV instead of P and FCF instead of E. Using that to determine my price target, I come to a fair value of $318.70 or 117% upside from the January 21, 2026, closing price.

Risks

The primary risk to my thesis is not AI or some sort of product replication, but whether Duolingo can keep growing at a rate high enough to justify its multiple. Duolingo has benefited from viral moments and successful marketing schemes like "Duolingo is dead". Unfortunately, viral moments are hard to drive repeatedly, so Duolingo may find it difficult to keep up the same level of organic user growth, even if their product continues to improve.

Another risk is the monetization asymmetry within Duolingo's user base. Duolingo does not serve third-party advertisements to free users unless they seek out things like extra energy or other bonuses. Instead, it serves its own ads to try to get people into the subscription. While this strategy certainly helps increase subscriber penetration, there is a certain subset of users that generate no revenue at all. It is likely that Duolingo will try to modify its freemium monetization model in a way that it can profit over even this subset of users, and if they do it ineffectively, it may alienate users and hurt the brand.

Conclusion

Duolingo stands out as a leader not only in language learning but also as the fastest-growing publicly traded consumer tech platform at an attractive valuation. Its habit-forming product, first-mover advantage, and extensive user data create a strong moat. Additionally, AI is likely to accelerate product improvement rather than harm Duolingo. The lack of direct competition is the cherry on top.

That being said, I would like to initiate my rating of DUOL as a "Strong Buy."

data.title: British American Tobacco: I'm Not Touching This At These Prices
data.description:
Over the past year, British American Tobacco stock (BTI) has undergone a considerable rerating. And by rerating, I basically mean a dramatic expansion of multiples. The P/E for 2026 is 11.9x, and the historical average is much lower than that. Basically, we are seeing this more favorable market sentiment for BAT, and this is reflected not only by the stock's performance and current valuation, but also by points such as Jefferies placing it as a top pick given the “shift toward a smoke-free portfolio.”

But my sentiment is still very negative. The numbers are not so consistent with this optimistic narrative, and in my view, even though the company is good and is making some progress, it still deserves a rating of a company linked to the tobacco industry, dependent on smokeables.

Therefore, my initial rating for BAT is a sell.

BAT’s Valuation

Not long ago, I wrote about Phillip Morris International (PM). Basically, my view is that despite the impressive execution in the transition to smoke-free products, there were several risks in the case, and yet the market priced it as a premium company, practically placing it in the status of a quality compounder, just like Coca-Cola Company (KO) and McDonald's (MCD), since at the time, the forward P/E was above 20x.

The case of BTI stock is not quite the same. The problem here is precisely when we compare the fundamentals that I will mention in the next section with the valuation. Okay, we are still dealing with a stable company, which can be called a cash cow and is trying to make a real shift, but there are too many risks to attribute such a large multiple expansion.

As already mentioned, its multiple is not as rich as PM's, and this is well justified, since PM is at a much more advanced stage of transition. But note how, in several multiples, BTI is already trading at a higher level than Altria (MO), a company that, despite also having a very high dependence on smokeables, carries a more premium status due to its history, geography, premium brands, and very high margins.

But the worst part is when we compare BTI with its own history. We are talking about a stock whose 5-year average P/E ratio is ~8.6x. Therefore, this multiple being at 12.6x (2025) is an increase of more than 40%. Note also that this rerating of the stock has also significantly compressed the dividend yield, which was another strong point of the company. You bought knowing the risk, but at least you had that cash flow up front through a high single-digit dividend yield.

This is one of the points to consider. If the company fails to meet market expectations and continues to deliver more modest results, how much could the drawdown be? If the multiple returns to something close to 10x, we are already talking about a correction of ~20%.

BTI: Only This Growth?

One of the points I thought about when I saw this expansion in valuation was: the company must have made considerable progress in the last year, especially in the direction of transitioning away from dependence on combustibles.

Well, that's not quite the case for BAT. In the consolidated results, revenue in euros rose 1.8%, and the adjusted EPS rose 1.7%. Without much news, the company continued to be what most expected and delivered numbers that it was already delivering before. It’s also worth mentioning that the growth grade is a D-.

The problem for me was “New Category Revenue.” The company called the performance “quality growth” in this segment, but revenue growth was only 2.4% YoY. Giving credit to BTI, the margin improved significantly, and this caused the return on investment to reach 21%, which is already a very satisfactory number. But note in the table below that we are still talking about a new category revenue that still represents only 13% of revenue.

It is interesting that the company is showing that this new part of BTI can be profitable. But for a multiple expansion of this size, I expected to see much more, especially in terms of topline, since a dependence of more than 80% on combustibles still does not remove the main risk from BAT, nor does it change the company's profile.

Other Compelling Points and Other Risks For BAT

Giving the company some credit, there have been significant advances, and this needs to be recognized. For instance, compared to the beginning of 2021, BAT already has three times more consumers for its modern oral products, which currently account for most of the revenue in the new category. In fact, the gross margin for modern oral is already higher than that for combustibles.

In addition, revenue in the United States is returning to a healthier pace, with growth closer to the mid single digits (~4%), and this also has to do with the new revenue category, where some brands, such as Velo, grew 384% and gained almost 7 points of market share.

All of this needs to be recognized, since if the company manages to maintain smokeables at a reasonable pace, it may even be with 0% growth, but if it manages to gradually make this transition and dominate a share of the heated products and oral products market, it is already possible to envision shareholder value.

But at the same time, we have to remember that there are other risks besides valuation and an occasional multiple derating.

For example, this recent advance in the US, especially for new products, depends heavily on how this industry will develop, and the rules can change at any moment. The market should continue to mature in regulatory terms (especially for vapor products).

This also applies to the other players mentioned. Altria already has a very strong presence in the US, and competition here is certain. All of these peers are trying to transition to smoke-free products and win over new audiences, and the market is still in its early stages, so there is no strong guarantee that BAT will continue to gain market share, nor that it will achieve such good margins in a sustainable manner, both due to possible legislative changes and competition.

A second point is precisely the prospects for this market. Although smoke-free products have much better prospects than combustible products, it is still a market that I, personally, have my doubts about. It is a market with some growth potential, with an increase in the number of people who want to quit smoking and are looking for an alternative, but I do not think it is such a strong cultural trend to say that there will be secular growth, or even sustainable growth in this industry in the coming decades.

The Bottom Line

Considering all this, I don't think BAT is a bad company, but even so, my rating is a sell. It is not a sell rating that means I consider it interesting to short the stock, but rather a rating that shows that at this moment of more stretched valuation, uncertain future, and still timid growth, I prefer to avoid it. It does not seem like a good time to have it in the portfolio, and after this rerating, I would prefer to take profits.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SentinelOne: Competition Seems Fierce
data.description:
After going public in 2021, SentinelOne, Inc.'s (S) bull run was short lived. Fast forward a few years and the stock is miles below the IPO price of $35. Just in the past 52 weeks, there has been declines of nearly 40% and so judging by the stock price there is either something fundamentally wrong with the company or the valuation has corrected significantly. SentinelOne reported their latest earnings last month and so today I'll be initiating coverage with a look at their results among other things.

Below, it is shown that SentinelOne is creating valuable AI-powered cybersecurity solutions. However, with top line growth being on a clear downtrend and gross margin seeing some pressures, I believe that fierce competition in this space is weighing on their performance even if the TAM is large. Soft Q4 guidance is potentially another reflection of this. With the valuation having come down but still at a premium to the IT sector, I would say that SentinelOne is around fairly valued right now and is a hold.

Purple AI Drives Better Outcomes

Before looking at their financial results, let's take a look at how AI is really driving value creation. One of SentinelOne's key offerings is Purple AI and in the above infographic it is shown that client outcomes have been improved significantly. Figures like 60% reduced chance of major security incidents and 55% quicker security threat mitigation shows that their core AI solutions are creating very real value. As a result, 3 year ROI for customers is reportedly a whopping 338%. In today's age of cybersecurity threats, incidents can wipeout large amounts of profit or capital and so the value proposition of advanced AI tools, like the ones SentinelOne create, is likely robust. At the end of the day, the value a company brings to the table is highly important and in that regard I would say they seem pretty well positioned.

Singularity Platform Offerings

In terms of their Singularity Platform as a whole, there is widespread implementation of AI in their solutions as well. As stated above, this includes generative AI and agentic AI. You can also see that they have added new solutions to AI & Automation, Data, and Threat Services. This expansion in portfolio is likely in response to expanding and evolving customer needs and so I would say that they have been proven to be innovators in this space.

Market Size Offers Opportunity

Lastly, quickly before we get into their Q3 results, let's talk about the size of the market. As shown above, the TAM is currently over $100 billion with areas like data analytics leading the way. Given the size of the company right now and the rate at which they are pushing into AI cybersecurity, I would say that they have the potential to capitalize on significant opportunities moving forward. The estimates above are 2025 forecasts and given the trend of cybersecurity incidents, I believe the TAM has a good likelihood of further expanding in the upcoming years.

Q3 Growth A Little Sluggish

For FY2026 Q3, SentinelOne generated total revenues of $259 million and that represents a growth rate of 23%. While this YoY increase isn't bad per se, you can see in the above chart that top line growth has overall been on a downtrend for quite a while.

Even though some slowdown is excusable due to the larger growth base of the business, I do find the relative sluggishness to be somewhat worrying. This could be a reflection of stiff competition in this space. With the amount of effort they are putting into AI, you'd probably expect better business activity and so there could be some disappointment. In terms of performance versus analyst expectations, they were able to beat by a razor thin margin of $2.73 million to show some very mild outperformance in the quarter.

Lastly, ARR is usually a better measure of longer term business momentum but in Q3, the growth rate for this metric was also 23%. This is a slowdown from the previous quarter's 24% ARR increase and so again, there seems to be some sluggishness for SentinelOne.

Customer Metrics

While there is no doubt that there is some sluggishness on their top line, customer metrics show adoption of their solutions have continued at a respectable pace. There was a 20% increase in $100K+ ARR customers YoY and customers that have adopted 3+ or 4+ solution categories have shown a large increase in the past 24 months. In my view, the considerable growth in customers that have adopted multiple solutions suggests that SentinelOne's value proposition does remain strong even if revenue growth could be better.

Margins Largely Improve

Overall, SentinelOne's margins are in good positions. However, it should be noted that while a gross margin of 79% is still exceptionally high, it does represent a bit of contraction from the year ago quarter. This is potentially another sign that competition in this space is quite fierce. Let's face it. Currently, there's no shortage of companies offering next-gen AI cybersecurity solutions and while SentinelOne's products are providing great value, I believe the relatively large amount of competitors is denting their growth and probably their gross margin as well.

In terms of the other figures above, operating margin jumped into positive territory as a sign of good corporate effectiveness and the sizeable jump in FCF margin is an indication that cash generation is looking good. For the bottom line, SentinelOne managed an adjusted EPS of $0.07 which beat expectations by $0.02.

Government Contracts

Earlier this month, it was reported that SentinelOne received GovRAMP authorization at the High Impact Level. This is really quite a big deal as it means that the company can now enter into contracts with U.S. federal and state agencies. This of course can lead to better growth moving forward. Andrew Howell, SentinelOne's VP of Government Affairs, stated that

Achieving GovRAMP Authorization at the High Impact level is a significant milestone because this opens the doors for state and local government organizations to deploy state-of-the-art security, which provides better protection for the most critical data and infrastructure.

While near term financial impact isn't likely to be significant, I believe this development does allow for more opportunities down the road. This should add to the TAM size we discussed earlier and so now it's likely a matter of execution.

Q4 Guidance Is Soft

We'll focus on Q4 guidance with just one quarter to go for the fiscal year but it should be mentioned that SentinelOne didn't really raise full year expectations despite beating on both the top and bottom line. The $1.001 billion expected for revenue is slightly above the previously provided midpoint but the 78.5% for gross margin is at the lower end of the range provided back in Q2. The 3% expectation for operating margin is unchanged.

For Q4, they are projecting revenue growth of 20% YoY. That would be a deceleration from Q3's already relatively sluggish growth rate and so there should be some concerns regarding business activity. As for gross margin, they are expecting 77.5%. That would be a notable dip from the prior year period's 79% and so the potential competitive pressures discussed earlier are expected to continue. Lastly, operating margin is expected to be at 5%. This implies around 4 percentage points of expansion and so at least their corporate efficiency is likely to be solid. As a whole, I consider Q4 guidance to be a bit soft as the continued top line deceleration and gross margin contraction are usually trademark signs of stiff competition. Post-earnings, analysts also considered the outlook to be soft.

Valuation Around Fair

As shown in the chart above, SentinelOne's forward P/S ratio has been on a slide since late 2024. While Seeking Alpha shows that the stock trades at a 32% premium to the IT sector in terms of this multiple, I believe the 4.852 reading shows the valuation is around fair.

In today's article, I found a company that's offering innovative and competitive AI-powered cybersecurity solutions. This should be able to justify quite a high valuation. Why is the valuation contraction justified then? Well, even though they are competitive, this space itself seems to a highly competitive one and you can see that from SentinelOne's slowing growth and gross margin pressure.

In my view, a premium to the IT sector is also justified as the TAM is large and the door is now open for them to pursue government contracts. Therefore, I would say that the valuation is much more reasonable than it has been and is a good reflection of both the opportunity and challenges the company faces.

Recap

To sum it up, SentinelOne is an innovative company that provides value-creating solutions in a highly competitive space. The problem with that is despite a large market, growth is slowing at a potentially worrying pace. At this point, due to a combination of factors discussed in this article, I view SentinelOne's long term prospects in a cautiously optimistic manner and I believe that the valuation currently captures that correctly. Therefore, I have decided to initiate the stock at a hold rating.

data.title: Exail Technologies: Europe's Defense Autonomy Push Creates A Compelling Buy
data.description:
European aerospace and defense stocks are in focus, driven by an increased need to increase European security autonomy supported by expanding defense budgets. In a prior report, I covered TKMS, which is a market leader in conventional submarines. In this report I am adding Exail Technologies (EXALF) to my coverage.

What Exail Technologies Does

Exail Technologies was formed in 2022 when two French technology companies, namely ECA Group and iXblue, merged. ECA Group was founded in 1936 and focused on autonomous underwater and marine robotics, while iXblue, founded in 2000, specializes in navigation and photonics. The merger brought two complementary technology portfolios together at a time when defense weapon systems and capabilities are rapidly being modernized.

The company is active in three main areas:

  • Autonomous Systems & Robotics: Provides underwater drones, unmanned surface vessels, and mine countermeasure platforms.
  • Navigation & Sensors: Provides inertial navigation systems and motion sensors with applications in the land, sea, air, and space domains.
  • Photonics & Aerospace: Provides optical components and fiber-optic technologies as well as communication systems and space instrumentation.

The core of the business is autonomous naval vehicles and navigation systems, but we are increasingly seeing demand across other domains increasing. So, the company is an aerospace and defense company focusing on providing a technological edge in autonomy, intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance, as well as navigation and fiber optic solutions. The company has seen its order intake surge from €220 million in 2021 to €612 million by H1 2025, with more orders expected for 2025. Revenues during the first quarter were €220 million, indicating 35% growth while order intake surged 279%. It shows that the company has a growing revenue path ahead.

The Risks and Opportunities for Exail

As a French company focused on robotics, autonomy, navigation, and photonics technologies, Exail is positioned well to benefit from increasing defense budgets. The crisis regarding Greenland seemingly has been defused for the time being. However, at the same time, the comments we heard at the World Economic Forum indicate that power balances are set to shift. Europe will have to step up its game, and it is already doing so, and the crisis in Greenland only amplified the need for that. New technology capabilities are critical for improving the European security and defense capabilities.

At the same time, we should also be mindful about the risks. Not all companies are in the same stage to handle increased demand from the defense market. The major risk at this point is simply the dependence on defense spending cycles. In 2024, Exail derived 56% of revenues from defense clients and 44% from civil clients. I believe that in the years ahead, the share of defense clients will substantially increase. That will increase the dependence on defense contracts. That is marked as the highest risk in the risk matrix, and that is generally a good thing since it is also the biggest opportunity at this time.

At the same time, we note that as an enabler through technology, the company has to continue investments to maintain and expand technological leadership, and due to the high-technology nature of the company’s solutions, export controls may apply.

There is no real risk mitigation for that other than the company being able to ramp up volumes and revenues in the years ahead that should show that risks are managed and spread well.

Exail Technologies Initiates With A Buy Rating

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Note: Price targets are based on Exail’s listing at the Euronext Paris. Price targets are converted from EUR to USD.

Exail Technology stock is definitely not cheap when we consider the company’s median EV/EBITDA of 11.3x. Given the substantial growth opportunities in aerospace and defense and European companies being somewhat preferred to benefit from the increases in European defense budgets, I do not believe that an 11.3x EV/EBITDA can be justified for a company attempting to push the technology envelope. As a result, I am using the aerospace and defense peer group EV/EBITDA to set the price target. That also makes sense as we see that the company is currently trading more or less in line with the price target commanded by the earnings for 2025. Even when we do not pull in the 2027 earnings into the target window, we still see 48% upside with a $181.09 price target. That is enough to rate the stock a buy.

In terms of growth, we are expecting 23.1% compounded annual growth in sales measured from 2024. On EBITDA, we expect 31.7% growth, while on free cash flow, the compounded growth is less impressive at 4.1%. This is driven by growing capital expenditures and a high free cash flow in 2024, providing a difficult base to benchmark the growth against.

The company is expected to grow sales in parallel with margin expansion from 20.2% to 24.2%, marking an increase of four points. Operating and free cash flow are expected to dip in 2025, followed by a recovery in the years after. From a cash conversion perspective, we also see that 2024 was an outlier. Typically, cash conversion is 35%-40%. CapEx as a share of operating cash flows is set to decrease, but it will remain at elevated levels to support demand. The company is expected to see its net debt decline steadily, and this year, absent of any major M&A activities or capital expenditures beyond what is currently modeled, I expect the net debt to be slightly below the 2x-2.5x that I target for aerospace and defense companies.

Conclusion: Exail Stock Looks Attractive

Exail Technologies appears to be expensive when looking at the current EV/EBITDA or the median EV/EBITDA. However, we note that Europe is in the early innings of substantially growing defense budgets, and while spending will not solely flow to European defense contractors, I believe that we will see European countries select European defense contractors where possible to reduce reliance on the US for security solutions. Companies like Exail Technologies that push the technology envelope are positioned extremely well to capitalize on the current demand trends.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: S&P 500 Snapshot: Sour Start Leads To Weekly Loss
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The S&P 500 kicked off the shortened trading week on a sour note but managed to claw back a portion of those losses, ultimately finishing the week with a loss of -0.4%. The index is now 0.88% off its all-time high from January 12, 2026. Here is a snapshot of the index from the past week:

The table below summarizes the number of record highs reached each year dating back to 2013.

Here is a snapshot of the index from the past six months with a 50-day moving average:

S&P 500: A Perspective on Drawdowns

On October 9, 2007, the S&P 500 reached an all-time high, closing the day at 1565.15. Then on March 9, 2009, the index dropped ~57% off of its high from exactly 17 months before, closing the day at 676.53. This time period became known as the Global Financial Crisis. It took over 5 years before the index reached a new all-time high on March 28, 2013, where it closed out at 1569.19. The chart below is a snapshot of record highs and selloffs since the 2007 peak reached on October 9, 2007.

What happens if we take out the Global Financial Crisis? Here's a snapshot of the same chart above where the start date has been changed to the trough reached on March 9, 2009. Note the recent sell-offs in 2022.

Here are a few tables with the number of days of a 1% or greater change in either direction and the number of days of corrections (down 10% or more from the record high).

And here is a linear chart of the index since October 9, 2007:

Here is a linearly scaled version of the same chart with the 50- and 200-day moving averages. The index has been above the 50-day moving average since January 20th and above the 200-day moving average since May 12th. Additionally, the 50-day moving average has been above the 200-day moving average since July 1st.

S&P 500: A Perspective on Volatility

For a sense of the correlation between the closing price and intraday volatility, the chart below overlays the S&P 500 since 2007 with the intraday price range. On April 9th, the index experienced its largest intraday price volatility (10.77%) since December 24th, 2018 (19.10%). Also included is the 20-day moving average to identify trends in volatility. Over the past 20 days, the average percent change from the intraday low to the intraday high is 0.70%.

S&P 500 versus S&P Equal Weight

The S&P 500 is a market cap-weighted index which includes roughly the 500 largest U.S. stocks spanning 11 sectors. The S&P 500 Equal Weight Index includes the same constituents as the S&P 500, but each company is equally weighted at a fixed weight. So how do these two indexes match up against each other this year?

The S&P 500 is currently up 1.02% year-to-date, while the S&P Equal Weight is up 3.71% year-to-date.

ETFs associated with the S&P 500 include: iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPYM), and Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight ETF (RSP).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 01/25/26
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.
